title: One of the New Wonders of the World
text:
The Colosseum is one of the most famous and visited attractions of the Apennine Peninsula. The monumental structure of ancient times is located in the historical centre of Rome, in the heart of the Eternal City. It is striking in its grandeur and strong energy, makes you want to touch the massive travertine walls and is simply fascinating.
The Flavius Amphitheatre evokes twofold feelings. On the one hand, the Colosseum is considered to be the epitome of the once powerful Roman Empire. On the other hand, he is a silent witness and guardian of dramatic events about the distant past. It all began with the construction of the giant structure, when tens, if not hundreds, of thousands of captive slaves carved tons of travertine into stone quarries, continued with the cheers of the crowd at the sight of bloody gladiatorial contractions, and ended with 15 centuries of oblivion.
Nevertheless, in ancient Rome the Colosseum was of great importance. It was a gathering place for emperors, senators and commoners, plebeians and patricians, townspeople and villagers. Not only were there deadly matches, but also mass fun and sporting events. 
The construction of the Colosseum in 70–72 was started by the Roman Emperor Vespasian (9–79), who came to power following four rulers who succeeded each other in the course of one year. He was the founder of the imperial Flavian dynasty, whose representatives held the throne for only 27 years. But nonetheless, they are memorialized for centuries in the Amphitheatre of the Flavii.
Vespasian's son, Emperor Titus, continued the construction of the grand structure. This was mentioned by Dion Cassius (155/164 – 230) in his 80-volume work "Roman History". The author was able to elaborate and structure the documentary evidence of the past available at the time so thoroughly that he is quoted even by those who are deeply involved in research on events related to the period of ancient Rome.
After the construction of the Roman Colosseum was completed in 1980, gladiatorial battles and bloody hunts were held in the arena for a hundred days in front of jubilant spectators. Several thousand animals have been slaughtered during this time.
The Romans were delighted with the scale, spectacle and generosity of the rulers never before seen. The site was completely covered in sand. It was pre-painted so as not to scare those who came with an abundance of spilling blood.
In 217, the Colosseum was severely damaged by a massive fire, but it was restored. Gladiatorial battles were fought there until the middle of the fifth century, and animal baiting until the beginning of the sixth century. After that, the Amphitheatre gradually fell into neglect.
Sad times for the Flavian Amphitheatre came with the arrival of the barbarians. The arena became a cemetery and the vaulted arcades became part of the workshops and living quarters. In the 11th and 12th centuries the stone structure became a real fortress in which representatives of noble Roman families hid. However, in the first half of the 14th century, the Colosseum was forced to be placed at the disposal of Henry VII, Holy Roman Emperor. He in turn returned the structure to the people.
The partial collapse of the Amphitheatre was the result of a powerful earthquake in 1349. The falling stones quickly found use as building material for palaces, and when the pile was over, they began to tear down the massive walls further. Travertine was plundered by noble families, cardinals and even popes. When and why this mass madness ended is not known for sure, but it left its deep mark — the structure took on disfigured forms.

title: Colossal
text:
When one thinks of Ancient Rome, one of the first images that comes to mind is the Colosseum. It’s portrayal in movies of an immense structure where crowds of thousands watch gladiators duel to the death is a striking and memorable one, but in pales in comparison to the real thing.

The Colosseum sits in the centre of Rome beside the Palatine Hills and east of the Forum, the seat of Ancient Rome’s government. Construction commenced in 72 AD and took eight years, with completion in 80 AD. At the time of its construction, it was the largest amphitheatre in the ancient world with a capacity of between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators. 

It was mostly used for gladiatorial contests and other public spectacles including animal hunts and could even be partially flooded to reenact famous sea battles.  

The structure remained in use until the early medieval period or the dark ages (5th to 10th centuries) when it fell into disrepair and was even damaged by an earthquake. From then on it was used for various purposes including public housing, workshops, a religious order’s quarters, a fortress, a quarry and even a Christin shrine.  Partial restoration efforts commenced in 2011 and lasted for four years.

Today the Colosseum is one of the most popular tourist attractions in Rome. The literal throngs of visitors that can be seen here daily bear witness to this. There are numerous options one can have to see the Colosseum. Self guided tours with headsets or group guided tours that cover all the site, combine it with nearby sites, or specialize in one section such as the subterrain chambers. It all depends on what one wants to see, how much time they have and of course what they are willing to pay. 

Due to time constraints, we declined to take any of the offered tour options during our visit. We simply spent our time exploring the site  and grounds outside. From the Domus Aurea, the ancient gladiator’s training area and home connected by a tunnel to the amphitheatre and now a museum to the temple of Venus and Rome on the opposite side there is much to see.

Walking around the Colosseum one gets a sense of the immense size and its capacity, especially when you look up at the groups of tourists seen through the arches and realise how small they are in comparison.

All around the site one is going to find vendors offering both souvenirs and essentials such as water, selfie sticks and phone chargers. Mixed in with them are others. Sadly, like in any other attraction there are thieves and scams preying on the visitors. Violent crimes against tourists are rare especially in such a public place, however these same crowds attract pickpockets so one should be careful and always aware of their surroundings. 

One should also be wary of those offering “special deals” on entry tickets and tours. Many are legitimate representatives of authorized and licenced  companies , however not all are.  Sometimes that great deal isn’t, so best to stick to authorized and official vendors. Finally watch out for those dressed up as period gladiators who offer to pose for pictures with you. Check first to see how much this will cost prior, to avoid a nasty and costly surprise.

Due to its central location getting to the Colosseum is easy even for those not part of a tour. The aptly named colosseum Stop on the Metro (subway) blue line is across the street on the north east side. Numerous bus stops are along Via Celio Vibenna and Piazza del Colosso the two streets that encircle the site. Finally, the HOHO Bus stops here as well just west of the site near the Aqua Claudia a few minutes walk to the south.

title: A must-do in Rome
text:
There are many places you can visit in Rome on your own, without a guided tour, quite easily and enjoyably. The Colosseum is not one of them, especially if it's your first time there. A guided tour will get you access to skip the queue and provide historical and contextual information to make the visit more meaningful. We booked a "Premium Colosseum Tour with Roman Forum & Palatine Hill" with City Experiences and were pleased with it, but there are many companies that offer similar tours. The Colosseum was extremely crowded, but our guide did a good job of finding spots for our group of 13 to stand together and listen as he shared information through the headphones provided. From the Colosseum we walked up Palatine Hill to see the ruins there, and then down through the Roman Forum. You should plan on 2.5 to 3 hours to visit all three sites.

Practical information: You need to bring your government-issued photo ID, and you will go through security and metal detectors, similar to an airport. The name on your entrance ticket must match the name on your ID, again as with air travel. Small bags and backpacks are okay to bring in, as are water bottles, and there are fountains inside to refill them. There are a lot of steep stairs and uneven surfaces, and a ton of walking so wear comfortable shoes, and not flip flops. There are few shaded areas in the Colosseum and it can get quite hot - it's a good idea to bring a hat and water bottle. There are washrooms at the entrance, and again at the exit, but not in between so plan accordingly.

text:

We did pay 8Euro for a guided tour. This was excellent. Our tour guide was brilliant, she provided all guests with a small receiver and ear-piece so we could hear her as we toured the site. We were not rushed and had plenty of opportunities for photographs.
The tour takes an hour, and she gave us so much interesting information. Once concluded you can still stay and explore. There are steep stairs to climb but an elevator is available. 
The Colosseum is a must see, but do your homework first to ensure you have the best experience.

title: So amazing
text:
I've wanted to visit the Colosseum since I was a girl, since the first time I saw Audrey Hepburn, Gregory Peck and Eddie Albert riding past, walking through and admiring the Colosseum in "Roman Holiday".

We booked a group tour well in advance, putting all our stock into a company that claimed they'd be able to get us access to the Underground and all the other places within the Colosseum before moving us on to the Forum. 

They lied.

Still, we had a chance to visit the Colosseum, in all its grand glory. We walked the paths, felt the stones, viewed the floor, examined the fossils found and put on display for visitors to see. 

We listened to the tour guide tell story after story of the above-mentioned fossils, listened to him explain the history of the stones, the years the Colosseum has stood, the things it witnessed.

It was impressive. It was inspiring. It was...a lot. There's so much to see and experience. There's so much to view and read, so many things to take pictures of or stare at. And we missed a lot of it, just because of the tour, which was on a timed entry.

Would I go back? Absolutely. Would I go back on a tour? Or in August? No and no. It may be more difficult to get tickets outside of a third-party tour company, but I think the flexibility afforded by going solo may be worth it.

title: Roma
text:
The Colosseum in Rome is undoubtedly one of the most iconic landmarks in the world, steeped in history and showcasing the grandeur of Ancient Rome. Visiting this awe-inspiring amphitheater is a step back in time, offering a glimpse into the gladiatorial games and spectacles that once captivated thousands of spectators.

The exterior of the Colosseum is a majestic sight, with its towering arches and intricate stonework standing tall against the backdrop of modern-day Rome. The scale of the structure is truly impressive; it's hard to comprehend just how massive the amphitheater is until you're standing in front of it. The restoration efforts are commendable, allowing visitors to fully appreciate the site while maintaining its historical integrity.

Inside, the Colosseum's intricate network of underground passages and chambers where gladiators and animals were kept, adds a layer of intrigue and excitement. The main arena, though partially destroyed over the centuries, still exudes an air of ancient grandeur, with its tiered seating that once held up to 50,000 spectators. You can almost imagine the roars of the crowd and the intense atmosphere of ancient combat.

Audio guides or guided tours are highly recommended, as they offer fascinating insights into the history, architecture, and the events that took place there. Whether you're a history enthusiast or a casual traveler, the Colosseum offers a captivating experience that brings the ancient world to life.

One of the best ways to enjoy the Colosseum is by visiting early in the morning or late in the afternoon to avoid the crowds, especially during peak tourist seasons. The view from the upper levels is breathtaking, offering sweeping vistas of Rome and a fantastic photo opportunity.

Overall, the Colosseum is a must-see destination for anyone visiting Rome. It's an unforgettable experience that transports you to an ancient world of spectacle and power, and is a true testament to human ingenuity and resilience.

title: An unforgettable monument

title: incredible place but a few things to be aware of
text:
Visiting the Colosseum is one of those experiences that if you get the chance to, you do. It is fascinating and awe inspiring. There are plenty of view points and there is a museum inside with lots of interesting artifacts and information. However, there are a few reasons for me not giving the experience 5*, first of all we thought it wasn't very clear which parts you were and weren't allowed in ( a lot of restricted areas and areas you have to pay more for) which resulted in us being shouted at by staff, not a great start. Also, understandably there is a lot of security on the way in, not a problem, what erred me is how rude the staff are, they don't even speak they grunt. Okay, you can reason that working with so much of the public on a daily basis is not going to make you cheery but people dream of visiting places like this and you are treated like an inconvenience and its just a rather miserable atmosphere. We were expecting it to be busy, crowded even, but not quite that much so just be prepared and this may just be a personal thing but so many tourists are glued and i mean GLUED to their phones, videoing everything rather than taking it in and taking a few photos which holds up the ques even more and making people less spatially aware and considerate. 
All in all, it was amazing but unfortunately I do think that it is quite a challenge to not let others take you out of the moment and and dampen the experience.

title: One of the Seven Wonders of the World. Plan Your Visit Well in Advance
text: Undoubtedly, one of the highlights of a visit to Rome and arguably one of the greatest sights to see in the world. Completed in AD 80, it is the largest ancient amphitheater ever built and is still the largest standing amphitheater in the world. Even if you don't have tickets for entrance or have not booked a tour, it is still worth experiencing for the views of the exterior. The Colosseum is especially beautiful at night. However, try to get inside but this will take some pre-planning. You can purchase a combo ticket for the Colosseum and Forum online but must do so well in advance. You should rent an audio guide for a small extra charge, and this would be well worth it. If tickets are sold out, the next best option is to book a tour with a high-quality tour group. This may end up costing 6-8 times the price of ordering a ticket online, but this is often your only option as the tickets to the general public are gobbled up by these tour companies as soon as they become available. It's really quite a scam. Do not show up without a ticket or else you will be waiting in line for hours. Even if you have an advanced ticket, get here early as the security line can be a long wait and gets worse as the day goes on. Most importantly, don't visit on days when the Colosseum has free entry. This is often the first Sunday of the month but can vary. It will be a complete madhouse.

title: Masters of Rome for a day
text:
Visiting the Colosseum means immersing yourself in the atmosphere of imperial Rome, where the crowd cheered and gladiators fought for glory. As you enter, the imposing walls and the breadth of the arena make you feel small in the face of the greatness of history.
Walking through the stands and observing the remains of the structure, it is easy to imagine the roar of clashes, the roar of beasts and the wait before the fight. You seem to be inside a scene from Gladiator, immersed in an era of honor and gory spectacle.
Going into the basement is even more impressive: here moved gladiators and animals, ready to emerge into the arena to face their destiny. Every stone tells a story, every corner carries with it the weight of time.
The Colosseum is not only a monument, it is a living symbol of the history of Rome. An unmissable experience for anyone who wants to feel the beat of the past under their feet.
I recommend booking in advance to avoid unpleasant queues. They're very organized.

title: Incredible building
text: The Colosseum is one of the most iconic and majestic monuments of Rome and the ancient Roman Empire. title: Excellent - go you won't be disappointed
text:
If you like history , spectacle, walking, and like Gladiator you have it made!!
The Colosseum is so impressive, unique and thought provoking. When you look down in the arena you see the very confined and cramped conditions the gladiators and animals were kept before facing death in the arena. A perfect labyrinth of passages overlooked by the colossal stands holding the likes of Caesar and his cohorts but also the general public too. 
There are lots of artifacts to see and indicate how the people used to live in this environment.
The entrance also includes the the grounds of the archaeological park containing so so many Roman remains and relics. It is impossible not to be impressed and overwhelmed by it all - some of the surviving structures are massive - how did they build this - now it would take us so so long to do - once we got planning permission!!
Before going we had read on the internet that you have to , HAVE TO, prebook tickets or you won't get in. This is nonsense. Maybe at the height of summer you might have to buy a little in advance, but we walked up and purchased tickets from the ticket sales and were in in 10 mins for 20 Euros each. The ticket sales on line are much much more and charge a fee for the transaction - do not be caught out by this. Only buy from the official sales point - there are fake ticket sellers on the street who press you to buy from them - again double the price.
Excellent - go you won't be disappointed

title: The world in amphitheatre
text:
As we approach this architectural colossus we feel that our time is not as evolved as we sometimes think. To enter it is to go back almost two thousand years, with a mix of feelings: on the one hand, the admiration for the architect of such a majestic work, taking into account the time and its entire structure, which can only be better known with a guided visit, without ceasing to pass through the underground; on the other hand, the revolt for the atrocities committed in this space, where thousands of people have died treated as neither animals should be treated, all in favor of imperialism and the power of a few over all others.
Only this visit should serve to evaluate the positive side, which is the imposition of the work, the quality of the architecture, its preservation of almost 2,000 years, what it represented for the Roman empire and for humanity, as well as for the current world and, thus, we are rendered and all the time we spend there is always little to be able to give us for satisfied to the point of not wanting to return.

title: Must see ancient wonder of the world!!
text:
The Colosseum in Rome is known for being the world’s largest amphitheater ever built and largest one standing in the world!  

Built between 72-80 AD on the site of Nero’s Golden Palace. It only took 8 years to build and notably no concrete was used to build the main structure. 85,000 people could fit in it! 

It’s where everyone went to watch gladiator fights and mock sea battles. It had 24 manual operated elevators to transport animals. It has 80 entrances and underground tunnels and chambers called Hypogeum. One of the 8 wonders of the world and amazing both inside and out!! Very amazing to see the architecture and walk on the path of past victors and losers!

title: Awe inspiring Grandeur , architectural marvel !
text:
The Colosseum in Rome is an awe-inspiring symbol of the Roman Empire's grandeur and a journey back in time where you can see that cultures across the continents were flourishing. Its colossal arches and architectural brilliance is a treat to visitors, offering a true sense of history within its walls. 

Highly recommended to buy tickets online which is around Euro15 and saves some time as there could be anyhow long queue even with tickets in hands, but not to worry it moves really fast and you can enter the remise in some 10-15 minutes in general. Reaching there in the morning hours should be preferred like 830am to beat the long groups that start pouring in by around 10am. Colosseum is open for varying number of hours depending upon the season with longest being around end of March to Sep (830am-7pm), although pls check it before you head towards the wonderful place.

Reaching the Colosseum is convenient, with many bus routes available from various parts of Rome. Buses that pass by the Colosseum includes multiple routes and easiest would be to ask your Hotel desk and they will guide to the nearest point to take the bus. You can also easily reach the Colosseum by taking Metro Line B to the Colosseo station. I would recommend having a bus as it gives you a real view of the city as well.

Nearby Attractions: Palatine Hill is a short walk from the Colosseum. The Arch of Constantine is also nearby, situated between the Colosseum and Palatine Hill. You can have a few moments of peace after the Colosseum and get some rest at Hill with a sceneric view.

In Summary - The Colosseum provides a unique and thought-provoking experience. You can admire the architecture of both the Colosseum and the Palatine Hill and enjoy walking up to the viewing platforms through the gardens. The views from the upper levels are fantastic.

title: Colosseum - (un)surprisingly impressive
text:
This place is an absolute must-see. Before our trip to Rome, I wasn’t sure if I wanted to wait in that long line and if the inside would be interesting—was there really much to admire beyond its sheer size? Although I’m a history lover, I hadn’t had the time to read up on this particular building and doubted whether it would impress me once I was inside.

I have to admit—I was completely wrong. The Colosseum is absolutely worth visiting and admiring, not only from the outside, but exploring from the inside. It’s essential to either read about it beforehand or take advantage of the informational stands inside during your visit.

It truly is an awe-inspiring feat of engineering.

title: In the summary of the history of Rome
text:
Describing the Colosseum in a post makes no sense. 
I just want to say something that maybe a lot of people don't know. 
Without the Colosseum there wouldn't be many historic buildings.
The marble of the façade and some internal parts of the Colosseum were used for the construction of St. Peter's Basilica and also for civil buildings such as Palazzo Barberini. Fallen into neglect, for a long time the amphitheater was in fact used as a source of building materials. So until the 18th century, when the love for the ancient vestiges of Rome returned. It is estimated that over time only one third of what was the original construction remained.

title: Well worth it
text:
After weeks of Internet trawling for the best option for a tour I decided just to turn up, which was around 1330 on a Friday, and chance my arm by waiting in the queue. 

On seeing the throng of hundreds of people with the group entrance being completely congested I allowed a local 'rep' to give me his speil about their guided skip the line tour. 

When adding up just the basic price of admission plus the Palatine hill I decided to pay €66 for myself and 2 children which I think was reasonable.   

The meeting point 10 mins later was well organised, with our guide, Christina, giving a comphrenhesive explanation of the do's and dont's with the meeting point and time for the Palatine, later on. 

We skipped the huge line and swiftly went inside. Her knowledge was extensive with a fun aspect. She also had a book showing us what the facts she was explaining. I thought my history and knowledge was varied but I learnt a lot. So a big thanks. After her tour we had over an hr to explore privately. 

The Colosseum is well preserved with the restoration kept to a minimum, thus not really impeding our experience. I could feel the history and imagined what life was like back then, with the guides help. 

I've visited many important historical sites but I was impressed by its preservation despite its age. When comparing it to Athens it's a no brainer. 

My children enjoyed the tour and would recommend it highly. 

We then met a more colourful guide. An expat American called Dan. Due to his humoured style my children enjoyed his tour due to his a animated interaction, despite a brief yet heavy downpour. As my children are from Scotland this wasn't a problem. 

The Palatine is an extremely important piece of Roman history where Rome actually rose from the ground up and continued its success as a hub due to the emperors insistence. 

The buildings are mainly ruinous but if you let your imagination go free its a wonderful experience. With the views being a good trade off for the long walks. 

A magical afternoon had by all.

title: Timing is important
text:
We bought timed tickets for 8:30am for Colosseum and Forum (joint ticket cost about 18 Euros per person). Visited the Colosseum first. At 8:00 am the queues are already forming and the tour groups (and their leaders) starting to get into position, but the wait wasn't too long and we moved quite quickly. 
There's more to see on the inside than we expected and maintenance and restoration works are ongoing. Very helpful info boards at various places.  We used the lift (yes. there is one, but it is tucked away out of the main area and not generally available). There are lots of steps between the entrance level and the upper levels though, so be prepared. 
By the time we came out the whole area was absolutely heaving and very busy (it had also got quite a bit warmer), so pick your time carefully.

title: Reliving History and Fiction
text:
It is always amazing to visit sites well known from history, literature and popular culture.  This was no exception.  We marveled at the skill and knowledge of the architects and builders and paid tribute to the slaves who did the back-breaking work.  
A week before our visit, I booked on-line, 24 hour tickets with timed entry to Colosseum, entry to Forum Romano “at any time”.  “Any time” is the time one reaches the head of the queue, regardless of having tickets or not.  Entry to the colosseum early afternoon was prompt at the exact time stated on the ticket.  Rick Steves audio was helpful for both
We visited the forum before the colosseum.  There is an uphill slope to the entry point for the forum/Palantine hill complex, then downhill through the remains of ancient Rome to the, now grass covered, site of the forum itself.  It would be hard going in a wheel chair, one flight of steps had a lift beside it which was not operational.  We didn’t attempt Palatine Hill, which is a good climb.  Did not see any toilets on the way to the forum.  
Any food options I could identify on-line required a time-consuming uphill walk, so we took a picnic and ate sitting on one of the stone bollards in the Colosseum forecourt.  After entering the colosseum we made use of the toilets on the ground floor and took the elevator to the first floor where we could appreciate its size and the complexity of operating the site.

title: An absolute must
text:
We took a guided tour of the Colosseum which, of course, was great. We even got to see the underground areas of the arena which isn't always allowed. We learned a lot, and it was fascinating to see the various rooms and holding cells while learning about the mechanisms the Romans used to release the gladiators and animals onto the arena plus create the "special effects" to make the games entertaining. 

Did you know that the plebeians (common people) did not have to pay to watch the games. They were paid for by the politicians and 1%ers as a way to "buy" favor with the plebeians. We saw a still-legible wall inscription which was basically this dude advertising that he paid for this game and to vote for him later. 😂 Not much different than politicians these days!

title: Stunning
text:
I visited the Colosseum as part of a combined Colosseum and Forum ticket that cost €18. This turned out to be an excellent deal.

I had booked for 9am and, despite believing the queues would be huge, I got inside in next to no time. There are lots of friendly faces about to ask questions if you're lost on where to queue, but it is also well signed.

The experience itself was wonderful and included sites I'll not forget. The Colosseum is a stunning piece of ancient architecture. It really blows the mind away.

You follow a set path, with small deviations, that takes you through a museum area with lots of info on the history of the Colosseum, before heading up to the second level of the tour, and all the spectacular views. There is a lot of reading to do, but lots of interesting items and images to see. There is also a small exhibition on a site of neolithic man in Turkey that I found very interesting.

The one big con with the attraction is the sheer number of people within the Colosseum at any one time. It's like marching through a thick soup of people. Despite that, most people are understanding of who is around and you can get to the places to see the best views and take the best photos for your memories.

Let's be fair though, it's normal almost anywhere to see so many people nowadays.

There's also a friendly seagull on the second level. I think he was just hungry though! I didn't have any fish handy!

title: Dajeeee Rome

Its majesty and grandeur leave you speechless, and every corner tells stories of gladiators and epic combat. The visit is exciting and well organized, with expert guides who enrich the experience with fascinating details. A must for those visiting the capital, absolutely not to be missed!

title: Amazing Visit
text:

on our own. Our guide had the right balance of providing information and allowing us to take in the
huge arena and take pictures. A guided tour also meant that we skipped the lines.
Our guide started us at the Palatine hills at around 10.30am, visiting the Colosseum after, which
seemed to be the right way, as the queues for the Palatine hills were huge when we left and the
Colosseum was relatively speaking easy to get round with fewer crowds around lunch time.
Whilst the weather was comfortable warm, it would be a very different experience in the hot
summer, and with more crowds. The Palatine area does not have much shade, so would recommend

title: A place both magnificent and monstrous.

title: Closseum in Roma
text:
Review of the Colosseum in Rome

We visited the Colosseum last week, and it was a truly fantastic experience. Everything was perfectly organized – from the entrance to the tour itself. Unfortunately, I was on crutches due to a leg injury, but I was pleasantly surprised by how accessible the site was. There was an elevator that took me to the upper level, which made my visit much easier.

An unexpected bonus was that, on our way out, we were given an alternative exit due to my mobility issues. This route led directly to the arena, allowing me to take a peek inside, which was an amazing experience!

Overall, our visit was absolutely wonderful. The staff was very friendly and helpful, making the experience even more enjoyable. The Colosseum is definitely worth visiting, regardless of any mobility challenges.

title: Colosseo ticketing scam is rife. Staff is enforcing exact name match with ID.
text:
Got denied entry because my online ticket has my initials instead of my first names. The young man insisted that my exact full name should be present on the ticket, as it appears on my ID, regardless of the ticket validity, of my proof of payment, etc.

I went to the booth to buy another pair of tickets, talking to staff about this nonsense. They insisted that I buy new tickets, even if my valid ticket had a minor inconsistency on the full name part. Then, I was able to enter without the staff even looking at my names. It does strongly feel like the colosseo staff is aware of scamming/scalping websites leveraging the colosseo's popularity, and actively participate in that scam. They also know about the many online scalpers but don't bother taking them down or closing their APIs to them.

The site of fantastic. What engineers did 2000 years ago made me forget how present-day Rome residents are abusing their legacy.

title: Interesting - One and Done

March 2025 it was in various degrees of renovation, as is much of everything in Rome.  The crowds were significant, even in early March.  I enjoyed walking around it in the early morning and at night when the crowds were gone.  It was interesting to learn the Romans removed the marble that, no doubt, made it all the more impressive.  They used it for the Vatican.  So there's that....  I'm glad we went but it's definitely a "one and done".  We did go in the Gladiators Entrance which was interesting to see the perspective from the "floor".  Since modern stadiums are so large, it didn't seem as imposing.  But for that period of time, its quite impressive.

title: A Bit Disappointed
text:
We had a tour of Colosseum on our own, without a guide. But we booked our tickets via Tiqets days before our trip to Rome. It was a wise decision because honestly, if we were there without any prior preparation, we wouldn't know what to do, where to go, how to find the ticket office: it is that crowded, even in March!! However, Tiqets office was quite far from the Colosseum (nearby the Roman Forum entrance), so the walk was a bit exhausting and on top of it, though we bought the tickets with arena access, the day we were there, we are told arena access is not possible due to overbooking. We are still waiting the reimbursements for that part of the ticket. 

Anyway, other that these 2 setbacks, entrance to Colosseum was easy thanks to Tiqets tickets: we didn't have to wait in the queue (entrances were time based anyway), security checks were pretty swift as well. But the Colosseum itself was a bit disappointing, especially having been to famous El-Jem in Tunisia where you can access all parts of the arena which makes the experience all the more unforgettable. In Colosseum all you get is the scuffle of masses. 

So, in case you're not that much into the Colosseum, watching it from outside will also suffice. Palatine Hill and Roman Forum are definitely much more interesting.

title: Beautiful
text:
Visiting the Colosseum is like stepping back in time, to an era when the Roman Empire was at its peak. This extraordinary amphitheater, an immortal symbol of Rome, leaves you breathless from the first glance. Its majestic arches and grandeur tell stories of gladiators, emperors and screaming crowds.

Despite centuries gone by, the property retains incredible charm. Walking inside makes you imagine the sound of swords, the roar of the crowd and the adrenaline of ancient shows. The view from the upper level offers a unique glimpse of the Roman Forum and the eternal city.

Tip: buy tickets in advance to avoid long queues, and also spend some time visiting the Forum and Palatine Hill, often included in the ticket.

title: “An unforgettable experience between history, beauty and wonder”
text:
The visit to the Colosseum with my family was an extraordinary experience, exciting from every point of view. We were lucky enough to be accompanied by a private guide, exceptional for preparation, passion and involvement skills. Thanks to her, every stone, every arch, every detail of the amphitheater came to life before our eyes, transforming a simple visit into a real journey through time.

The route was impeccably cared for, with clear explanations, never boring, full of anecdotes and curiosities that have been able to fascinate both us adults and our son. She was very good at calibrating the information, always making it interesting and suitable for our group, patiently and competently answering all our questions.

Walking through the majestic arches, overlooking the arena imagining the splendor of ancient Rome, discovering the basements where the incredible organizational machinery of the shows took place... all this was made even more special thanks to the infectious passion of our guide.

I highly recommend anyone who wants to live a complete and engaging experience to rely on a private guide: it really makes a difference and allows you to fully appreciate the grandeur and history of a unique place in the world.

An experience that has enriched, excited and will remain among the best memories of our days in Rome.

title: Amazing
text: Amazing place, couldn’t recommend going here more, a step back in time and learn about Ancient Rome. Truly breathtaking place.

title: Amazing it's still standing!
text:
Well if you come to Rome, you've got to see the Colosseum; so you need to plan ahead and prebook your tickets. It is one of those tourist attractions that is always heaving with people. We pre-booked which allowed us to jump the queues and go straight in through security at our allotted time.

When we visited there was quite a bit of scaffolding around the structure which funnelled everyone on to the one side near the entrance. Once inside it was quite difficult to pick up the route to follow which takes you through the museum exhibits. There is a lift for anyone with mobility issues. 

There are viewing points around the building at various points and levels with opportunities for photos. It is amazing how big the arena is and how much has survived over the centuries, so it's well worth a visit.

It remained difficult to pick up the route again when leaving the building- rather like a maze, it seemed like there were signs for several exit points. This was not our favourite site in the city but as I said, you still have to visit while in Rome.

title: Terrible for babies and breastfeeding
text:
Review with a baby.

The actual colosseum is fantastic. A wonder of architecture.

I visited with my baby (8months) and I just found the whole experience very difficult.

They suggest baby carriers rather than prams. Take your pram. It’s a nightmare in a carrier as there’s nowhere for babies to shelter from the sun whereas a pram would have the sun shade. It’s like going to a football match, shoulder to shoulder with people so I think a pram would be safer for baby.

The floors were smooth enough for any stroller and there are lifts and if you can find them separate baby/pregnant/disabled exits.

The staff were tired and unhelpful. The signage was crap.
Toilet queue was massive.
There are little to no benches.

The website advertises a place for breast feeding. There is no signage to this and when I asked they said it doesn’t exist anymore.

I know it’s an old structure but I feel the capacity of people entering is too much and you can’t really enjoy it because of how busy it is.

The official website is confusing too. I bought a ticket and didn’t get access to what I expected. I asked if I could pay the extra €4 or upgrade or buy a new one and was told no.

With that alone you could spend all day in there if you wanted to. We only spent a couple hours so we didn't see every corner of it and read every single plaque. title: Wonder of the ancient world
text:

One way trek around so make sure to take the side path for the arena floor before venturing up the stairs to the second floor which is not obvious. 
An audio guide is thoroughly recommended. If you do not order one, use Rick Steve's audio guide which is fantastic and really brings the 90 minute journey to life

title: Avoid website Colosseum by Walker.  Purchase tickets from Parco Archeologico de Colosseo for 18 Euros
text:
This is a wonderful historical site.  We visited on a Tuesday and I believe I could have bought tickets at the site.  

I was scammed and bought tickets from a website Colosseum by Walker which sold an entry ticket plus audio guide.  Neither my husband nor I were able to download the audio guide over WiFi or using our data plan by our phone carrier.

The entry ticket is 18 Euros and the Colosseum by Walker only offers a combo ticket plus audio guide which costs an extra 35 Euros.  I reached out to the email and phone number provided with my purchase to ask how to download the audio guide several hours before my visit.  There was no reply by email and the phone number may be bogus (no one answers).  Basically, I was scammed out of 70 Euros on top of the base ticket price for an audio guide which couldn’t be accessed and no assistance by means listed in the email sent to me.  

There is plenty of information at the site.  I recommend to either get a guided tour or be sure to get just the entry ticket for 18 Euros/ per person.

I wish I had avoided Colosseum by Walker site and saved my money for enjoying Rome.

It gets very busy, so go early in the morning or book skip-the-line tickets. Also, wear comfortable shoes—the ancient stone steps can be uneven.

Absolutely worth the visit for history lovers and anyone fascinated by ancient civilizations.

title: The Colossuem in Rome
text: The Colosseum in Rome is a must-visit for history enthusiasts and travelers alike. This ancient amphitheater, built nearly 2,000 years ago, offers a fascinating glimpse into Roman history, from gladiator battles to grand public events. The site is impressive, with its towering stone walls, underground tunnels, and well-preserved architecture. While it can get crowded, especially in the summer, it's well worth the experience. Be sure to buy tickets in advance and try to visit early to avoid the busiest times. A truly unforgettable part of Rome’s history!

title: Sight-seeing.
text:

That said, the experience was a bit hampered by the massive crowds and long lines—even with timed tickets. It felt a little rushed and hard to fully enjoy the atmosphere with so many people around. Also, parts of the site were closed off during our visit, which was disappointing.

Overall, it’s absolutely worth seeing for the history alone, but I’d recommend going early in the morning or booking a guided tour to make the most of it.

title: Genuine history - but wow what a lot of people
text:
Like much of Rome, you know you are walking in history when you enter the Colosseum.

I bought tickets through the official website and made it through a relatively short queue with no issues - you have to scan your ticket three times before you actually get in. Oh, and ignore any suggestion you can’t take backpacks, you can. You will need to put them through an x-ray machine, but you can take them in.

The lower floor (not the Arena floor) is pretty forgettable, but the second floor has all kinds of artefacts to look at before you get to gaze on the Arena itself. A genuine piece of ancient history, pretty amazing.

But, be prepared for a LOT of other people if you are going in peak season. Like much of Rome (Trevi Fountains, Spanish Steps, etc) it was swarming with people. One good thing about going inside is you get away from all the people trying to sell you bracelets, water, sun shades - if they try to be friendly, just smile and say “no thankyou” because (whatever they say to start with) they do expect money.

Would go again - but off-season!

title: Colosseum… A Wonder of the World!!!
text:
Named (sadly) after Colossus of Nero, the 100 ft tall Bronze statue of Emperor Nero that was later fortunately decommissioned…. also known as the Flavian Amphitheater, as it was constructed and inaugurated during the reigns of Flavian Emperors Vespasian, Titus & Domitian…. Completed in 80 AD, this was the largest amphitheater ever built, and remains the largest one standing till today…. the outer walls are covered in three levels of Doric, Ionic & Corinthian columns, and each level used to have 80 arches out of which only 31 have remained intact…. apart from the vastly documented Gladiator fights, it hosted mock naval battles too… it has survived dreadful fires at least 3 times throughout it’s history, & at least 4 severe earthquakes….
Shot at the Golden Hour before sunset…

title: Iconic, Surreal, and a Must-Do in Rome 🏛🌕 (Book in Advance)
text:

Know Before You Go:

There are different types of tickets:

Regular entry (what we did)

Attic access (rare)

Underground tours (very limited and often sold out fast—book well in advance if you want this).

It took us about 1.5–2 hours to explore the inside. It was very crowded, but still surreal to be there—standing where gladiators once fought. The scale is jaw-dropping.

There are at least two designated smoking areas inside. And yes, they have to post signs warning not to carve your name into the walls... sadly, some still have. 

Your Colosseum ticket also includes entry to Palatine Hill and the Roman Forum—don’t skip these!

Final Thoughts: Grand, unforgettable, and truly iconic. A bucket-list experience we’re so glad we did!

title: Pre-bought tickets and still didn't get in!
text:
Entrance to the Colosseum is completely chaotic! We pre-bought tickets for the Roman Forum and Colosseum weeks before our trip and chose a specific time from the timeslots that were available. 

Entrance to the Roman Forum was fine, just long lines depending on the time of day and which entrance you choose. I can recommend going away from the Colosseum and using other entrances where the line is usually much shorter.

The Colosseum tickets say to arrive 15 minutes before your time slot. We had chosen 12:15 and arrived just before 12:00, only to be met with a massive line with absolutely no organisation. We went to the front where people were actually being admitted and had to distract one of the staff scanning tickets to try and get information - absolutely nobody is there to answer questions or help, despite the large crowd of people who were confused and unsure. Apparently everyone just joins the same single line, regardless of the time slot they have reserved, and I watched her admit people who are booked at 13:00 or even later! What is the point of time slots if they are not adhered to? When I asked what happens if we miss our timeslot, she could not give a clear answer and just told us to join the line. We ended up not even bothering, as by our estimate the line would take at least 40 minutes and the entire thing is in direct sun.

I can only leave a 1 star review as our tickets didn't even get us inside. A waste of time and money in regards to the Colosseum. There seems to be far too many questionable aspects about visiting the Colosseum, from scam ticket websites to zero organisation or consideration for people's experience. Whoever is responsible needs to do something to clear up what should be the icon of any trip to Rome.

title: Back to Ancient Rome
text:
A unique experience in time!

Knowing that such significant moments of history took place there makes the experience even more special. Being in that space, feeling the grandness and imagining everything that has already happened there, is something words cannot fully describe. A real dive into history!

title: Book direct, go very early or late in summer
text:
We booked through the official website. Bots and scalpers get a lot of them so you have to book quick if you want any of the popular options (guided tour, underground or upstairs access etc). A batch of tickets are released 30 days before and the rest exactly 7 days out. So plan for this and book on the day your tickets are released. 

We opted for the slightly less popular 24h general access tickets (with the SUPER pass add on for the Forum). We didn't feel we missed out too much not being able to go on the rebuilt arena floor section, you can still see it and you still get access to that level. The underground is probably very interesting but only with a guide. You can still see down into much of the excavations. We also saw a cat hunting in the underground area!

Note: All tickets are 'skip the line tickets' now because all are timed entry - there is still a line and a bit of a crush to get in. Don't bother lining up if it is more than 10-15 minutes before your entry time - we saw half the "line" getting turned back and told to come back closer to their time anyway. The line is mostly just for security and moves quickly. 

I actually felt a bit sorry for people with a guide here (opposite for the Forum). Being able to move through at our own pace with a guide book and a bit of pre-reading was great and the crowds are so overwhelming that I wouldn't have wanted to be stuck in a group as well. 

Most people seemed to just be dashing past the museum section but it was really interesting. Very good signage here and lots of interesting history of the place and its uses over time. Take the time to read and take in the info here.

We went early (first entry) which may have helped with the crowds a little. However if I was doing this again I would go very late, do the Forum and Palatine Hill in the morning, have lunch/a nap, then go back for the Colosseum in the late afternoon. This is because the Colosseum is mostly inside, while the Hill is large and outside. If you're not planning to do the Forum or not on the same day, then first thing is best because it is a little cooler.

title: Great place to visit. Get tickets in advance.
text:
My visit to the Colosseum in Rome was an absolutely unforgettable experience, despite a few key considerations that future travelers should be aware of.
Firstly, it was very hot. We visited in July, and the heat was quite intense, especially during the peak of the day. If you can, I would highly recommend avoiding July entirely, or at least planning your visit for early morning or late afternoon to escape the scorching sun.
Secondly, and perhaps most importantly, get your tickets in advance! The Colosseum is incredibly popular, and it gets very, very busy. We saw incredibly long queues for those trying to buy tickets on the day, and having pre-booked meant we could bypass a significant portion of that wait, which was a huge relief in the heat.
We wisely combined our Colosseum visit with a tour of the Roman Forum, and I can't recommend this enough. The two sites truly go hand in hand, offering a comprehensive glimpse into ancient Roman life. Our guide wove a fantastic narrative that connected the gladiatorial spectacles of the Colosseum with the political and social heart of the Roman Forum, bringing the ruins to life.
Overall, despite the intense heat and the sheer volume of people, we had a fantastic time. The sheer scale and history of the Colosseum are breathtaking, and it's an absolute must-see when in Rome. Just be prepared for the crowds and the heat, and plan ahead with those advance tickets!

title: Colosseum Must See
text: We booked a private guide through Tours by Locals for this attraction.  We saw the Arena floor, 1st and 2nd levels.  Our guide, Pietro, told us about the history of the colosseum and its part in Roman life. There was much more to the games than just gladiators fighting and wild beasts devouring victims. The aim was entertainment. Gladiators were expensive to train and often fights were simply to first blood as it was a huge financial loss to the training academy if they died.  Not all animals were for fighting. At the start of the game there were parades with rare and exotic creatures to amaze the crowds. Often twists were used to switch things up. One time a bunch of lions were set loose and then hundreds of rabbits turned loose. Rather than the lions eating the rabbits, they had been trained for months to catch the rabbits alive and bring them back to trainers.  The colosseum took 8 years to build, 5 for construction and 3 for decoration. It had a moveable roof made of sailcloth that was operated with ropes to ensure shade in the arena and the seats. The floor of the arena was a false wooden floor covered in sand. Animals went in and out of the arena on elevators that lifted cages up to trap doors. The floor has long since rotted away leaving the underground complex exposed.

title: A Must-See – Book in Advance!
text:
An incredible monument, absolutely worth the visit! Be sure to buy your tickets in advance to avoid long queues. With a standard ticket, you can explore almost the entire Colosseum, except the underground tunnels beneath the arena floor.

We also got access to Domus Aurea with the same ticket, which was a great bonus. If you want a deeper experience, you can upgrade your ticket – just ask at the ticket office a few meters from the main entrance.

Arrive early to beat the crowds, especially in high season. The structure itself is breathtaking, and if you’re more than casually interested in history, consider booking a guided tour or using a mobile app guide.

We saw everything we hoped for, and would highly recommend a visit!

You can't come to Rome and not visit the Colosseum, it is an incredible piece of architecture and one of the highlights of a city packed with history.
There is a wide variety of ticket options so I recommend you do some research based on your needs - city pass with other locations included, guided tour or not.
We made use of one of the city pass options as we were stopping for a few days and would be making full use of the other locations included.
Either way I would recommend booking a time slot as we did not have to wait very long to get in.  There was some blockage at the gates with people trying to get in outside their allotted times and these were quickly turned away by staff.  You will need to bring ID along with your ticket and be prepared to be searched on entry.
We opted for a non-guided tour and found the site easy to negotiate with the option to take things at our own pace.  It was a particularly hot day but there is plenty of cover inside to take a breather.
The site itself is beautifully preserved and my utmost respect for the city for leaving it unspoiled by the modern distractions of unnecessary gift shops and signage.
Due to its scale it can be hard to capture the Colosseum in its entirety - you can get some dramatic pictures from the Forum and Palatine Hill which were included in our ticket option and also well worth a visit.

title: Colosseum is a must see in Rome!

title: [No Title]
text: The Colosseum is to Rome what the Eiffel Tower is to Paris. It is Rome's nearly 2,000-year-old symbol of power and glory. Located east of the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill, it was dedicated in 80 AD by Emperor Titus in a ceremony that included 100 days of games, it remains the largest amphitheater in the world. Oval in shape, it measures 189 meters long, 156 meters wide and 50 meters high, about the height of a 12-story building. It also featured 80 entrances, 36 trap doors and seating for 50,000 spectators. It was known as the scene of thousands of hand-to-hand combats between gladiators, of contests between men and animals and even larger contests such as mock naval engagements. Construction began between 70 and 72 AD during the reign of Vespasion, on the grounds of what was Nero's Golden House. The artificial lake that was the centerpiece of that palace complex was drained and the Colosseum was situated there. The Colosseum was built of stone and concrete, using a complex system of barrel vaults and groin vaults measuring 620 by 573 feet overall. The spectators were shielded from the sunlight by a massive velarium or awning. Supporting masts extended from corbela built into the Colosseum's top and hundreds of Roman sailors were required to manipulate the rigging that extended and retracted the awning. The Colosseum was treated as little more than a quarry for more than 1,000 years. All the marble seats and decorative materials disappeared. Earthquakes in 1349 and 1703 caused serious damage. Preservation began in the 19th century and a restoration project was undertaken in the 1990s. Today, about 1/3 of the Colosseum remains. Enter through the Gladiator's Gate and stand on the arena floor where warriors once fought. See the Colosseum's underground, the catacombs, the dungeons, where animals were  kept, where prisoners were kept. Go to the second floor and enjoy the vision from the Emperor's balcony. The Colosseum has long been one of Rome's major tourist attractions, drawing close to 7 million visitors annually.

title: A must-see when in Rome!
text:
We bought standard tickets for €18 per person on the official website. This ticket includes entry to the Colosseum, Roman Forum and Palatine Hill on the same day. You have to choose a timeslot for the Colosseum, so you can visit the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill before or after. 

Our timeslot was at 14.15, so we visited the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill first. Entry was quick and easy. We had to show our passports with our tickets. After that you go through an airport security style check. We spent around 3 hours there. It was incredible to walk around this ancient city! It feels extra hot here, so beware of that! There are water fountains everywhere to top up your water bottle, but there are also vending machines for a drink or snack. Lots of toilets around as well, which were clean. The paths can be uneven and there is some climbing to do, so wear good shoes!

Entry to the Colosseum was the same; again showing your passports with your tickets and going through security. Beware there are only toilets right after the entrance. Be ready to queue and the toilets aren't the cleanest. The Colosseum is wonderful to see and inside you can walk all the way around it. There are also some displays and there is a giftshop inside. We spent around an hour here. 

Great value for money and highly recommended to visit both!

title: Amazing experience
text:
Stepping inside the Colosseum was a true delight, especially on a scorching summer day. The shade within the structure offered a welcome relief from the intense heat outside, making the experience all the more enjoyable.

Overall, the visit was memorable and well worth it. However, I would advise against spending extra on arena floor tickets. While it may sound exclusive, the arena itself doesn’t offer much more than what can already be seen from the upper levels. In fact, you get a better perspective of the entire structure from above, and the ground floor lacks any particularly unique features.

It’s also worth noting that much of the access to the tribunes (upper seating areas) was restricted during our visit, which limited the ability to fully explore those vantage points.

Despite these minor drawbacks, the Colosseum remains an awe-inspiring site, rich with history and atmosphere. A standard admission is more than sufficient to appreciate its grandeur.

title: A must see
text:
We, a family of five (grandparents, parents and a teenager) ventured out, in the midst of a heatwave, to visit one of the most well known sites in Rome: The Colosseum. 
This amazing structure is almost 2,000 years old and is steeped in history, not just for the games which it is most famous, but also why and how it was built. A feat of engineering, it is mind blowing that it still stands today considering the many times it has needed to be repaired over its existence due to natural disasters or otherwise. Within the walls are artifacts and information and many places to pause and overlook the arena. Despite the heat, we found the Colosseum visitor friendly, staff were very helpful, and visitors respectful of the space and each other. Overall, I would recommend the Colosseum as a must see in Rome.

title: One of the most amazing heritage sites in Europe!
text:
The Colosseum is one of the premier heritage sites in Rome and in the whole of Europe. Built in the late 1st century, it is also known as the Flavian amphitheatre after the dynasty during whose reign it was completed. Its capacity was at least 50,000 people, which means that it could hold 2.5 times as many people as the O2 Arena in London. It was used primarily for gladiator combat and other spectacles such as animal hunts and mock sea battles, though it gradually declined after the fall of the Western Roman Empire.

Visitors to this attraction can enjoy great views of the arena, which you can only visit with a more expensive ticket, as well as of the nearby area. There's also an exhibition about the Colosseum's history on the second floor and the second floor balcony offers you some of the best views of the Colosseum itself. Unfortunately, some of the other more interesting parts of this site, such as the Gate of Death and the underground tunnels, can only be visited with the more expensive tickets.

Standard tickets to this site cost €18.00, though there are various discounts and there are also options to buy tickets with audio guides on various websites. The ticket also grants you access to the Roman Forum and the Palatine Hill and you can easily spend a whole day exploring these three heritage sites. I suggest you set aside at least an hour and a half for the Colosseum but if you decide to go for the more expensive ticket that grants you entry to the arena too, you might need more than two hours. If you're really interested in history, make sure you give yourself at least 2-3 hours to explore the Forum and the Palatine Hill. Unfortunately, I decided to visit the Colosseum in the morning and see the forum and the hill in the late afternoon, which meant that some parts of these sites were closed and I couldn't see everything. Therefore, it might be a good idea to visit the Colosseum last as it seems to close later than the Forum and the Palatine Hill.

title: Experience doesn't match magnificent building
text:
The Colosseum is the greatest building in the world in one of the greatest cities in the world. And yet this experience is so poor. Driven by profits and ramming as many people through as possible. The owners should invest in a proper experience, they badly let this down. 
Work to do;
- Metro station next door is horrible and dirty. A shambles for the most important station in Italy. 
- Colosseum booking process is a madness scroll of hundreds of websites, some of which are just scams. Thousands of confusing deals on tickets being advertised is just ridiculous. 
- Mad busy. I expected it to be busy of course, but the lack of direction is astonishing. Follow the crowds is the only real way to get round. 
- Totally lacking an immersive experience. Where is the education, where is the view of what this looked like when it was built. 1 TV showing a video on loop is not an immersive experience. Go to Ephesus Museum in Turkey and you will experience what the Colosseum should be like, at minimum. 

I would of course go again. This place is incredible. But the modern experience is simply unacceptable.

Then I found tickets online from a secondary tour operator for over £100 each, so ticket touts are profiteering on the tickets due to a loophole where you can change names once. Saying that- if you can- its brilliant!

title: Worst Colosseum Ever
text:
Now I rarely ever give terrible reviews. But this place was possibly the worst of all tourist attractions we have ever been to (and we’ve been to a lot).
Now this is somewhere I have wanted to go all my life, but just never got round to it… but on a road trip through Italy so had it on the list.
So… you have to book in advance. Be very aware of this. There will be places they can get you a ticket, but it’s up to 10x the entry cost. The Colosseum doesn’t care about this… is all about the money to them. (Disappointing as it’s the only place in Italy we found like this).
So unless you hit on or pay and extortionate amount you aren’t going to get the best tickets with the best views or access… we resigned ourselves to this. That’s ok. 
But we managed to get tickets for 3.15 on a Sunday. We arrived on time to be ushered to another gate and entered at 3.20. Despite speaking with someone on gate, someone checking tickets, someone else checking tickets, someone on ticket desk and someone on security, no one mentioned they were closing soon. 
Took a few photos and kids went to toilet and by the time we went to go upstairs (3.45) all gates and lifts were locked. Staff explained they were closing. 
A number of rude, sarcastic staff claimed we arrived too late, until I showed them time stamped photos… then they just rolled eyes, walked off sniggering and staring as a group. We were not the only visitors shocked by the attitudes of staff, indeed it was a talking point by a number of people we spoke to as we left. 
Tried to email their team and got a very nasty email as a reply, again lying about time we arrived. I said I had footage (of us waiting for a lift!) and they replied saying I was breaking law if I posted it! 
Certainly a hostile and angry  bunch of people there, don’t quite understand why as have never experienced anything similar like this at any other tourist attraction anywhere in Italy.

title: First-Time Guide to the Colosseum in Rome
text:
If you’re visiting Rome for the first time, the Colosseum is an absolute must-see. Located about a 20-minute walk from the main train station, it’s easily accessible for travelers arriving on the high-speed train from Florence.

Take your time to stroll around the entire exterior—doing a full 360° walk offers plenty of photo opportunities from different angles. To explore inside, it’s best to reserve tickets in advance. If you forget, don’t worry—official tour guides nearby often provide skip-the-line options.

As with any major tourist attraction, stay alert to pickpockets and keep your belongings secure, ideally positioned in front of you.

For bird enthusiasts, the Colosseum offers a unique bonus: look out for rose-ringed parakeets and, occasionally, monk parakeets nesting along the outer ring.

A visit here is highly recommended for history lovers, photographers, and even bird spotters alike.

title: EXTRAORDINARY MONUMENT OF ROMANITY
text: In the center of Rome, adjacent to the Roman Forum and the Palatine Hill, there is this extraordinary Romanesque monument: built almost 2000 years ago it has arrived to the present day, unfortunately with some missing parts. It was an amphitheater used for gladiator shows and other public events used until the 6th century and then became a quarry of material. Polycentric oval in shape, it has a wonderful exterior facade almost 50 meters high in travertine, with a triple series of 80 arches framed by semi-columns on three orders, with an upper attic with Corinthian pilasters crowned by shelves and punctuated by windows: a charm! The magnificent inner elliptical arena had a stepped marble and wood cavea at the top, divided into horizontal sectors by audience category: from senators and families in the lower sectors, to the lower classes of the plebs in the upper sectors. Entrance was via ladders, except for emperors and authorities who had reserved entrances. In the impressive basement of the arena there were several galleries for the custody of beasts, props and elevators. The visit of the monument is perfectly organized, since you can buy the entrance ticket, including the visit of the Roman Forum and the Palatine Hill, also online, avoiding the long queue at the ticket offices and having a dedicated entrance. Price (€ 16,00), organization, friendly and helpful staff, make the visit truly magnificent! The ticket allows you to visit the first level of the Colosseum, turning the entire ellipse: it was extraordinary to admire this wonder from various perspectives, all calmly walked for a time of about 90 minutes. Exciting and spectacular experience of a unique and wonderful place and rich in history!!!! Must visit, super recommended!!!!

title: An eternal memory of the Power of Ancient Rome

(Flavian Amphitheatre to be precise) our visitors the architectural mastery and the constructive devices that compose it prove the power of Rome at that time. title: A Roman Holiday: Where Faith Meets History
text: The long-awaited trip to the Vatican was more than just a vacation; for our Filipino family, it was a profound spiritual pilgrimage that brought tears to our eyes the moment we stepped into St. Peter's Square. Walking through the hallowed halls, we felt an overwhelming sense of peace and connection to centuries of faith, a feeling impossible to describe. Transitioning from that spiritual high to the ancient, bustling heart of Rome was an incredible contrast, but one that left us equally breathless. Seeing the ruins of the Roman Forum and the Palatine Hill painted a vivid picture of a colossal empire that shaped the world. But nothing prepared us for the moment we first laid eyes on the majestic Colosseum rising before us—it was simply monumental and truly overwhelming. Standing inside the amphitheater, imagining the raw drama and history that unfolded there, gave us chills, making us feel tiny yet connected to a powerful past. This architectural marvel was the absolute highlight of our Roman holiday, a place where history’s echo seemed loudest. We left Italy with our hearts full, cherishing the memory of a journey where faith and history intertwined so beautifully for our entire family.

title: The famous Colosseum
text:
The Coliseum 

The Romans loved their entertainment.  I discovered, as a result of Gladitorial games, The Atlas bear (Ursus arctos crowtheri), Africa's only native bear species, was driven to extinction. 

Flamma (lit. The Flame) was a Syrian gladiator under the Roman Empire during the reign of Hadrian. He was one of the most famous and successful gladiators of his time.

Flamma was awarded the rudius, or wooden sword that granted freedom, four times; but, each time he refused this freedom and chose to remain a gladiator.  The number of fights Flamma engaged in is a lot higher than most gladiators. Many have lower numbers like Purricina Iuvenus who fought 5 times or Glaucus of Modena who fought 7 times. Flamma had fought 34 times and won 21 of them.

Julius Caesar gets blamed for the widespread proliferation of gladiator games.  He had decided that pleasing the public with more games made himself more popular.  It wasn’t always slaves, criminals, and prisoners of war who made up the gladiator population. There are apparently instances where the patrician(upper wealthy) class would become a gladiator. I think this might explain why “the gladiator” became so glorified. Three out of every five gladiators ended up losing their lives in the arena.

Given its size and complicated entry procedures, it's best to take a guided tour, which typically comes with entry to the Roman Forum.

There are some exhibit areas that are worth exploring to check out how the Colosseum has changed over the centuries.

Overall, this is a can't-miss attraction in the Eternal City.

text:
Visiting the Colosseum is an extraordinary and deeply exciting experience. This iconic monument sublimely represents the grandeur, power and ingenuity of ancient Rome. Its grandeur strikes from the first glance and, once inside, every architectural detail tells centuries of history, culture and civilization.

Walking through the arches and levels of the amphitheater allows you to imagine the life of the time, the shows and the central role that the Colosseum had in Roman society. The organization of the visit is well structured and allows you to fully appreciate the historical and artistic value of the site, also thanks to clear and in-depth information panels.

title: Have the Colosseum to yourself.
text:
The Attico is the way to see the Colosseum. They only sell tickets for 6 people every half hour. There were never more than a handful of people, the views are amazing, and we were never rushed. The view of Rome in the background was amazing! We were able to spend 45 minutes walking at our own pace, taking amazing pictures, and watching the people below and in the Underground, being herded like sheep. 

Tip: Make sure to purchase your Timed Entry tickets in advance (30 days exactly & at the time you want to go, on Rome time, not home time). Do a couple practice runs the day before the day you want to go. The ticketing page is a bit odd. These aren't the easiest tickets to get, but it can be done. We got ours on the third attempt of the day.  You won't regret getting the Attico tickets!

title: Shocking. Be supportive
text: Buy the ticket online. Go in time. Remember that there are several security and entry controls. Be prepared to go through things and not slow down. Be supportive of moving inside the Colosseum. title: Family visit – mixed despite the beauty of the site
text:
We visited the Coliseum with our family (2 adults and 2 children aged 11 and 8).
Booking made on a site we usually use, everything went very well on this side. The meeting point was clear and convenient, and our taxi dropped us off right in front. The explanations in French for how to proceed and where to go were very well done.

However, once they arrived in front of the Coliseum, it got a bit complicated. Despite the designated entrance on the ticket, inside you must mostly follow the movement of the crowd, with the impression that everyone goes to the same place. It’s not very intuitive.

We booked the “Full Experience” ticket including the arena floor, the 1st floor, the Palatine Mountain and the Roman Forum. But the lack of clear indications on the spot has disoriented us a bit.

The audio guide is good in French, which is good, but we found it a bit sketchy and not always very fluid, especially with children.

The site remains impressive and full of history, it is obviously an emblematic place to see. However, we found that there was a lack of presentation and support to help visitors better understand and fully enjoy the experience.

A nice visit despite everything, but which could be better organized and more immersive.

title: Horrible dirty DO NOT VISIT
text:
Enough is enough with the Colosseum! This ancient relic is nothing more than a dirty, crumbling eyesore that’s been hanging on by a thread for far too long. It’s a massive safety hazard, falling apart piece by piece, and yet we keep pretending it’s some kind of sacred monument. Newsflash: it’s old, worn out, and frankly, it’s time to condemn it!

Why are we wasting resources trying to preserve a ruin that can barely stand? It’s a breeding ground for decay, with crumbling stones, graffiti, and years of neglect. Tourists come expecting grandeur but get dust, rubble, and disappointment. The Colosseum is a relic stuck in the past, dragging Rome down with it.

We need to face facts: this ancient structure is no longer fit for purpose. It should be demolished immediately to make way for a brand-new, state-of-the-art facility that meets modern standards of safety, accessibility, and technology. Imagine a venue that can host world-class events, concerts, and cultural experiences — something Rome can be proud of, not embarrassed by.

It’s time to stop romanticizing decay and start building the future. The Colosseum’s days are over. Let’s tear down the crumbling mess and rise up with a modern marvel that truly represents Rome’s greatness today.

title: THE worst customer service. Do not assume you are getting in the Colosseum if you book tickets in advance.
text:
I booked 2 tickets direct from the official website on 2nd Janiary 2025. I booked flights and hotels and was looking forward to our amazing trip to Rome,  for my wife's birthday on 3rd April. I received a confirmation email at the time of booking, and was advised my tickets would be sent a couple of days before my visit. 
Two days prior to the visit, the  venue cancelled the tickets and blamed me for not completing my personal details page at the time of booking. This was a complete lie.
On contacting them, they confirmed I had booked the tickets correctly and that they had been fully paid for. They then told me there was no longer space on my allotted date/time and the error was theirs. They stated that there was no other available spaces that entire weekend and they could not offer me an alternative. 
They provided no explanation.
How can that happen on an automated booking system? I have spent the best part of £3000 in flights and a hotel, for my trip to the Colosseum now to be cancelled 48 hours prior. 
Their customer service has been disgusting. They have finally offered a refund, but that's not what I wanted. I have been denied a chance in a lifetime  visit to this historic site by complete mismanagement and an awful  company. 

Just because you have booked and received confirmation, don't expect that to  mean anything. Its a lottery.

title: Disappointing, plan ahead but beautiful
text: Booked tickets on the official Collosum website which gave and allocated time slot to visit. You have to be in the queue to go in at the exact time otherwise you will be turned away. Our daughter is 18 and I didn't want to risk booking a free child ticket as you have to take your passport to go in. There is a discount ticket which is 2 euro for 18-25 year olds but it's just for EU citizens as the UK is no longer in the EU we got caught out here and were made to buy a full price adult ticket which they would not refund the 2 euros already spent. This resulted in a very disappointing day. This was our second time visiting and we really didn't enjoy our time this time around. We wanted to show our kids the history of the Roman empire but there is nothing to show as an example of what the place was like or what the Romans looked like. With modern technology and AI this could be easily done to help imagine what it was like. They had a screen which we stood and watched thinking it may spark something but it was a complete p*** take as it was just a video showing how they installed the lift!!! The place was crowded and there are lighting cables laying everywhere. The tickets included 24 hour access to the forum and palatine but you could only use this for one entry. Nothing to say how long it would take to see it all. We didn't have enough time to see all of it before our time slot at the Collosum. The forum is run down some of the signs are worn away so can't read them. The plastic wooden effect decking broken and not very good for disabled or people with pushchairs. I was very disappointed as this was the part of Rome that I really loved on our previous visit 20 years ago. We also walked around at night and the lights are really badly positioned and don't light up the areas properly with some things not lit up or lights shining in your eyes. Everything is gated and migrants trying to sell you tat everywhere. Rome although beautiful and lots of history is not what it was. It's dirty and I didn't feel safe especially at night.

title: A timeless wonder that never fails to amaze
