title: Spectacular
text: The most famous monument in the world and symbol of the greatness of Rome. The building was intended for fighting, gladiator games and simulations of hunting wild and exotic animals. It consists of four overlapping architectural orders; the first three are formed by eighty arches framed by half-columns, the fourth is divided into squares interspersed with windows. It could accommodate about 50 thousand spectators who could sit in the cavea. Despite the two thousand years of history, the imposing structure still transmits incredible power, you have a blast from the past. Beautiful view of the arena from the 2nd floor

title: Really impressive!

title: Rome caput mundi
text: The Colosseum is the symbol of the grandeur of the Roman Empire: Roma caput mundi. It is one of the "Seven Wonders of the World". The only one in all of Europe. It was completed in the year 80 AD and had a celebratory function. Put aside the important practical-competitive-religious-theatrical functions of the largest amphitheater in History. The function of the Colosseum was mainly mystical: it was to magnify the Roman Empire that stretched from Britain to Africa, from Spain to Mesopotamia and beyond, from the Atlantic to the Persian Gulf. And to magnify the most articulate and complex civilization in History. Where Rome arrived, it created Provinces entrusting the government to local governors and creating infrastructure with cutting-edge building techniques. Delivering art, culture and law. Going so far as to recognize and grant Roman citizenship also to "foreign" residents in the "conquered" provinces by fulfilling their requirements. Rome was a bridge between the Peoples of the World. It allowed the fusion of cultures and peoples. Roma caput mundi. Start. Domenico Di Paolo - Isernia (IS)

text:

Unfortunately, we didn’t quite know which paths and corridors to complete in which order, but I think we explored everything important.
There are also some vending machines that are already good to use in the high temperatures.

The Colosseum is easy to reach by metro, right next to it you will find the "Forum Romanum" and after a short walk you will also see the remains of the "Circus Maximus" and from there the "Bocca della Verita" (mouth of truth) is not far away.

There is an entrance control, also take your passport with you!!
Comfortable shoes are highly recommended and pay attention to your training while climbing the stairs;-)

title: Beware of Headout. Its a SCAM!!!
text:
If there were an option to give zero stars, that is exactly what Headout deserves.

I had meticulously planned a once-in-a-lifetime family vacation to Rome from California, something my family—especially my kids—had been eagerly looking forward to for months. Like most travelers, I did my research and found platforms like Headout, Viator, and others claiming to be authorized sellers for major attractions. Given how quickly tickets to iconic sites like the Colosseum and Pantheon sell out, I booked well in advance—over two months ahead—paying nearly $100 per person for the Colosseum guided tour and about $25 per person for the Pantheon.

Everything seemed legitimate. I received confirmation emails, created an account, downloaded their app, and had all tickets readily accessible. Headout’s instructions were clear about the meeting point and arrival time, and we followed them precisely, arriving early and checking in as instructed.
What happened next was beyond shocking.

We were bluntly told that our tickets had been cancelled—no explanation, no prior notice, no alternative arrangement. Just like that, our long-awaited Colosseum visit was gone. My children were heartbroken. What should have been a highlight of our trip turned into a complete disaster.

And it wasn’t just us.
There were multiple other families standing there—confused, frustrated, and equally devastated—who had also booked through Headout and were being told the exact same thing. This clearly wasn’t an isolated incident; it felt like a systemic failure or, worse, complete negligence.
Headout’s customer service was utterly useless. No accountability, no clear explanation, no urgency—just scripted apologies. They couldn’t even offer us an alternative slot for the Colosseum, not the same day, not the next day, not at all. Instead, they casually suggested a substitute tour of Palatine Hill—as if replacing one of the world’s most iconic landmarks with “some nearby ruins” was an acceptable solution. It was frankly insulting.
Unfortunately, the nightmare didn’t end there.
The same issue happened with our Pantheon tickets. Again, cancelled. Again, no explanation. Again, no resolution.

After wasting precious vacation time arguing with their support team, it became painfully clear that it was pointless. All they could offer was a refund—something they are obligated to do anyway. But a refund does not fix ruined plans, disappointed children, or lost experiences that cannot be recreated.
What price do you put on a once-in-a-lifetime family memory that was completely destroyed?

I strongly caution anyone considering Headout: book at your own risk. Having confirmation emails, app-based tickets, and advance bookings means absolutely nothing with them. We had everything in place, and still, we—and many others—were turned away.

Their customer service is cold, indifferent, and completely lacking in empathy. There is no ownership, no responsibility, and no genuine effort to make things right.
I am currently disputing the charges and seeking additional compensation, but the case is still open—and frankly, I am not hopeful.

Avoid Headout if you value your time, money, and peace of mind.

title: Colosseum is a must-see !
text:
Visiting the colosseum was one of the most amazing experiences of my trip! It was incredible to walk through the same paths where gladiators and ancient Romans once stood thousands of years ago. The history and atmosphere are unforgettable, and seeing the massive structure in person is absolutely breathtaking.
If you’re not doing a guided tour, I highly recommend purchasing your tickets directly through Parco Archeologico del Colosseo, the official Colosseum ticket website. Buy your tickets early because they do sell out quickly! They offer several ticket options, including the 24h Colosseum, Roman Forum & Palatine ticket, as well as Full Experience tickets with Arena Floor and Underground access.
The Arena Floor tickets are awesome because you get to stand where the gladiators once fought, and the Underground tour is fascinating to see the hidden chambers beneath the arena. This is truly a must-see experience in Rome!

title: A can't miss attraction ... but better with a guided tour
text:
My wife and I were booked for a guided tour of the Colisseum and surrounding Palatine Hill area, but because it was an Italian National Holiday and no English speaking guides were working that day, we were forced to tour the site on our own. Thankfully, we were able to keep our skip-the-line tickets and entry time. Otherwise we might still be standing in the enormous line waiting to get in. 

Once inside, were were able to spend as much time as we liked getting the full Colisseum experience both on the ground floor and upstairs (which can be accessed either by walking up stairs or by elevator). It's an amazing place, a must-see attraction for every first-time visit to Rome. And we got as much out of experience as we would there thanks to information signs posted throughout the Colisseum, but it would have been so much more informative and meaningful if we had a knowledgable guide leading us.

text:

I wish there was a better way but that's what you have to do. The ticket line for same day tickets is massive. If you have to stand in line, bring an umbrella for the sun as there is no shade.

There is a special place to enter that's marked outside the colloseum. When inside it's really easy to find the meeting spot. Fascinating tour. Also it brings you to a private area near the arena with no people except your group so you can see some really interesting things up close. title: City's landmark, business as usual, this time with an extra
text:
Impressive, yes; crowded, yes; tourist factory, yes and confusing the ticket system because with or without arena or lift; Domus Aurea which I missed it again (open only in the weekends, this was the palace of Emperor Nero and the Colosseum was a reason to erase Emperor Nero works on those days) and the super sites which I made it this time.  There are many super sites spread on Palatine hill and in the Forum area; most of them are not in open-air, is space limited and the delicate nature of them makes them kind of restrictive with weekly closing days. Visited them and I was impressed by beautiful frescoes located in many of them; Loggia Mattei ceilings (Renaissance XVI century), Santa Maria Antiqua church (earliest VI century) and the homes of Emperor Augustus and wife Livia (around 30 bC).

Just prepare to walk lots; when we were there, main access of some sites were closed due archaeological works so had to ask the staff alternate access and saw many other tourists turned away because they didn’t purchased the extra ticket to visit these sites.  

This is the 3rd time that I visit and still have the impression that I miss other attractions inside Colosseum/Forum areas.  Depending if history enthusiast or not, read and watch all info possible because it changes everytime due to closings for restoration/archaeological works and new openings of new sites, plan carefully if suit your personal interests.

title: Crowded and not very interesting. Disappointing experience
text:
By far the most disappointing experience in Rome.

Okay, it's huge. Okay, it's impressive that nearly 2,000 years ago people managed to build something on this scale. But here's the problem: there isn't all that much left of it.

Rome is packed with historic buildings that are more beautiful, better preserved, and far less crowded, yet tourists seem determined to ignore them while flocking here as if the Colosseum were the only thing standing since antiquity.

However, if you're planning to visit, here are some extremely useful tips:

1. Buy your tickets well in advance. We tried to buy ours more than a month before arriving in Rome, and the only tickets available were for Roma Pass holders. As a result, we spent €130 on two vouchers that we used only for the Colosseum and for five or six metro rides. In other words, we managed to turn public transportation into a luxury experience and wasted about €100.

2. Be very careful where you buy your tickets. The ticket market around the Colosseum sometimes feels almost as competitive as the gladiatorial games once held inside it, therefore, starting with 2026, authorities have cracked down on countless intermediaries who were reselling tickets at inflated prices.

3. Pay close attention to your entry time. This is not a mere suggestion. If you're late, you can say goodbye to your visit.

4. Be careful with the combined ticket, which allows you to visit both the Colosseum and the Roman Forum. Many tourists do not organize their time well, the ticket expires and they are left to admire the Colosseum only from the outside.

5. Read something beforehand or book a guided tour, otherwise, you'll spend most of your time looking at walls, arches, and more walls, trying to imagine where all the excitement is supposed to be.

6. If you want to visit the entire building, you will need additional tickets, so if you buy your tickets online, be careful which options you choose.

7. Bring water. If you don't, you'll end up buying it from street vendors nearby. Paying €5 for a small bottle of water is a wonderful way to experience ancient Roman engineering and modern Roman entrepreneurship at the same time.

8. Speaking of street vendors, don't buy the garbage that they are selling as "power banks". If you think you'll need a portable charger, buy a proper one before your trip.

9. Most importantly, keep an eye (and, even better, a hand) on your wallet. There are countless teams of professional pickpockets operating both inside the Colosseum and in the surrounding alleys. Even worse, the nearby metro station hosts one of the most efficient pickpocket crews I've ever encountered. I didn't take any photos, but a quick search on social media will show you plenty of examples of them at work.

Conclusion:

If you're a fan of Gladiator, you'll probably love the experience.

If you have plenty of time in Rome, it's certainly worth seeing once.

But if your interests lean more toward palaces, churches, museums, galleries, or simply exploring the city itself, and especially if your time is limited, you can admire the Colosseum from the outside, take a few photos, and move on. Despite its legendary status, you're not missing nearly as much as you might think.

title: Unmissable.
text: We went to Rome to celebrate my birthday there. The visit to the Colosseum was a must. A marvel. In my case, having reduced mobility, with accreditation, we had free tickets without lines....

title: A millennia-old experience... with an unexpected touch of modernity!
text: The Colosseum always takes your breath away; its majesty is indescribable and it is a must-see in Rome. Excellent organization and smooth flow of visits. The most absurd thing happened while I was taking photos: I activated an experimental Augmented Reality (AR) filter on my phone and the application had a major bug, "dressing" the amphitheater in a yellow lace pattern (I attached the screenshot; you have to see it because it’s hilarious!). An incredible mix of Roman history and modern digital short circuits. Highly recommended!

title: Absolutely a must-see at the Colosseum!
text: A guided tour of the Colosseum is a must in Rome; we had a great guide who could tell us many historical and modern stories. Be sure to book a tour well in advance!

title: Must See Marvel
text:
This is a must see.
The queues were quite short and we went almost straight in.
You're left to go at your own pace and wander round.
It's a stunning attraction - beautiful inside and out - and full of history.  We took our time to enjoy it as it was the highlight of our stay in Rome.
We didn't go on the arena floor itself because we hadn't  booked a ticket that includes that, but we found you could still see everything and there was no pressure to move on.

title: For 2000 years, a wonder of man
text: The Colosseum is Rome. Built in the Flavian era by Vespasian in 70 AD and completed by Titus in 80 AD. It is an emotion every time you look at it today; we think of the image of grandeur it conveyed 2000 years ago: standing 52 meters tall (now 48), capable of holding up to 87,000 spectators, with an oval shape and a perimeter of 527 meters, featuring an arena of 3357 square meters. A UNESCO World Heritage site, it is no longer just the symbol of Rome but a national symbol in the world. Absolutely worth visiting.

title: Extraordinary
text: The monument is worth visiting; it is a magical and magnificent place. It's a shame we could not see the outside due to the barriers (the arrival of the Giro and the next day being a national holiday), but the interior is truly impressive and majestic.

title: Nice place to visit
text: Good experience. The views from inside is amazing. Remember to buy tickets online else there is a huge queue for tickets at the counter.

title: Utterly sublime
text:
Been multiple times now!! The history is utterly breathtaking!

I have always sorted tickets before going just for ease, and have never had a particularly long queue time! 

Fully worth it!!

title: Don't use GetYourGuide
text: Do NOT use GetYourGuide! They canceled our very expensive colosseum tour at time of check in (we were early) and were completely overbooked which they told us. Use anyone else

title: Wonderful
text:
Even before realizing it, upon exiting the subway, the impact is almost unreal. 
You immediately find yourself in front of this imposing structure that, in an instant, transports you directly to the time of the ancient Romans.
It is simply a fabulous monument that, with its majesty, dominates the entire surrounding landscape.

title: the first thing to see in Rome: the Colosseum
text: A must-visit. It is difficult to obtain tickets two weeks in advance. One must plan ahead to avoid buying them from resellers at exorbitant prices. Plan for about 1.5 hours. There is no need to buy a ticket for the arena or the underground part; a standard ticket is sufficient (unless you want to see things up close).

title: Colosseum
text: Very exciting place.. beautiful for its history.. there were about 80 thousand Roma on a day of fight..

title: 3rd time still amazing!
text:
This was my third holiday to Rome and the Colosseum remains magnificent every time. It really isn't enough to see it from the outside, I would highly recommend going inside as you get to see the underground where all the animals and gladiators were kept. Not only this but there are multiple artifacts and information detailing the history of the colosseum which are well worth the read.
I took my partner who was very happy that there were designated smoking spots inside the colosseum.
We did the roman Forum first and he did make a comment that he found the Colosseum underwhelming after doing the forum first.
I recommend booking a month in advance doing the colosseum first in the morning between 9 and 12am then doing the forum in the afternoon especially if its your first time. 
Alternatively if you don't know where to have breakfast and your in a rush do the forum first as they have a café on site!
I would also highly recommend going for an evening walk through the city and walking past the Colosseum at night when its all lit up is an experience in itself, its quiet and safe so you can sit there and marvel while eating an ice cream together and really appreciate the moment.

title: magnificent place.
text:
The monument is definitely worth a visit....  
the interior is truly impressive and majestic.....if you stop by in rome, you can not miss this view!

title: #1 Attraction on Planet Earth!
text: What can I add that others have not? It’s magnificent! As is the Forum. Surprisingly breezy and shady inside! Our guide was through City Walkers. Allesandra was the best! She was an art historian who was born and raised in that very neighborhood, in the shadow of the Colosseum, the greatest historic site on Earth. Allesandra was an absolute expert in knowledge of the sites and in managing the crowd and tour. She’s spent her life studying its every detail, and we were so lucky she shared her knowledge with our family. The pace of the tour was fine. We started in the Forum and ended in the Colosseum. We had earbuds. I cannot stress enough how critical it is to bring water and a hat and dress in light, breathable clothing. I witnessed two elderly ladies attempting to haul big heavy bags with them. They didn’t make it. They did not have the stamina to make it through the 3 hour tour. It’s worth the hype. It’s worth the crowds. It must have really been something! I highly recommend City Walkers and of course, the Colosseum itself.

title: Perfect
text: Historic place that deserves to be visited. It is good to purchase the entrance ticket in advance because you can face a big queue.

title: Breathtaking
text: An amazing creation that still stands.

title: Make sure you walk around the Inside - recommend a tour
text:
Somethings are smaller and less impressive when you actually see it, but the Colosseum is not one of those.  You can walk around it from the outside for free, but you really need to go inside with a guided tour if possible. We used LivTours to see the  Forum and Colosseum. It included access the partial arena floor where the gladiators fought but not the lowest level where the animals were kept but you could see down into that area.

Bathrooms:  After you get through the long line (we were able to bypass it with the tour), there are some bathrooms after the entrance turnstile but there was a long, long line for the women's bathroom. Instead if you walk around the Colosseum and can find the back exit which is located down a hill/steps there are accessible toilets (10 am - 7 pm usually) located under the hill/steps. It is open to the public.

The main accessible entrance is located at the Sperone Valadier entrance, which is specifically designed for visitors with reduced mobility. Here, you can enter without stairs and receive assistance if needed.

Once inside, elevators provide access to different levels of the Colosseum.  Keep in mind the area around the Colosseum has the original large stone and cobblestone around it which make for uneven walking or pushing.

For our tour, we walked around the main floor then walked up the 50 steep steps to a higher level and then back down to the area floor. 

The things that stood out to me were:
- the Colosseum used to be filled with water for ship battles
- the elevators used to bring up animals to the arena floor - they could come up from several different locations
- The shade awnings (no longer there) that were used to provide shade to the upper levels where the common people sat. 
- in the Middle Ages, many people lived in the abandoned Colosseum since property ownership wasn't a thing. There are display cases with some of the artifacts found.

title: Tour Operator cancelled Tickets 3 days before Entry Date!
text: We booked entry tickets to the Colosseum to Include the Forum, Palatine Hill plus we added the Colosseum floor to our tour. The tour operator was RoamMad. Three days before our tour was to commence, the tour operator sends an email stating that he was unable to procure our entry tickets!!! It was very important to us that our son and 17 year old grandson visit the Colosseum. I then had to scramble to find replacement tickets. These tickets sell out quickly and tour operators buy up all the tickets, and charge premium prices for the entry tickets, even without a personal tour. I was able to only find 2 Colosseum entry tickets for just our son and grandson, no Colosseum floor entry tickets available. It is INCREDIBLY frustrating to think that we have our tickets and then to find out, the tour operator obviously either over sold the tickets they had, or decided to charger a higher price for our tickets. Do NOT book any tickets through tour operator RoamMad!

title: Magnificent experience, ignorant staff.
text: Magnificent experience at the Colosseum; touching a piece of history so important to our country is truly something unique! It's a shame about the experience with the staff, especially referring to the girl in charge of the elevators on the second floor for going down (curly blonde, a bit overweight, time of the incident 11:25/30). I should mention that I am a person with a disability and have a condition that does not allow me to exert physical effort, so I needed the elevator. There were no problems going up; the girl helped me, my mother, and my sister, and she was very kind. However, I cannot say the same for going down, as once we queued, the girl looked at us and exclaimed, "a bit too much." I explained to her that I needed to take the elevator, and she responded, "it's only for special necessities." I explained again (in Italian, and the girl responded in English) that I have a disability and that I NEED to take the elevator. Then she looked at me and my family and exclaimed, "do all three of you have a disability?" with a somewhat mocking expression. In the end, my mother and I were able to take the elevator while my sister had to take the stairs. 100% ignorance.

title: Even more impressive than expected
text: At the Colosseum, I was not only impressed by the size but especially by the thought of how old this structure actually is. You stand in front of it and think about how many generations of people have already seen it. The atmosphere around it is, of course, very lively, but as soon as you take a little time and look at the details, the place has a completely different effect. For me, it was less a tourist attraction and more a piece of history that surprisingly still stands in the middle of modern Rome.

title: Book Direct for the Arena Ticket
text:
We visited the Colosseum using the Full Experience Ticket with Entry to the Arena of the Colosseum, booked directly through the official Parco Archeologico del Colosseo website.

After finding many Colosseum, Palatine Hill, and Roman Forum tours sold out the day before, booking direct was excellent value at €24. Some third-party providers appeared to be charging significantly more simply to secure tickets, so it’s worth checking the official site first.

The ticket included timed admission to the Colosseum, entry to the Arena, access to the Roman Forum, Palatine Hill, Imperial Fora, SUPER sites, and exhibitions. The Colosseum entry time is specific, so don’t arrive expecting to enter well ahead of your slot.

The Colosseum itself is extraordinary: fascinating, powerful, and an architectural wonder. Standing inside and seeing the Arena level gave us a deeper appreciation for the scale and engineering of the site.

There is also a free official MyColosseum app for iOS and Android, with interactive audio guides in Italian and English. I found the format a little less engaging personally, but it’s worth downloading if you’re visiting without a guide.

Tip: book direct, reserve early, bring water, sunscreen, snacks, and comfortable shoes.

title: Family Tour
text: The site is a must-do; even if the crowds are shoulder to shoulder. We booked a tour that included Palestine Hill, the Forum, and the Colosseum. It was hot but the guide tried to keep us in the shade as much as possible. Bring a refillable water bottle because fountains are there for refills. Very informative. Can’t miss this!

title: Step back in time
text: Amazing to experience this walk round would probably be better with a guide. But an experience nonetheless.

title: A must visit
text: Fantastic experience full of history with every step.  I was shocked at the size it's massive definitely buy tickets online in advance we did it without a guide but there was lots of information to read.

title: Excellent
text: Brilliant experience booked through Get Your Guide which was very easy and our guide Aphrodite was an excellent guide that pointed out all the main feat of the areas we visited would use get your guide again without doubt
